title: Comparators and ordering

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Comparators and ordering; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.claim: Ordering algorithms and containers require a comparator that behaves as a strict weak ordering, including irreflexivity and transitive equivalence classes.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Ordering algorithms and containers require a comparator that behaves as a strict weak ordering, including irreflexivity and transitive equivalence classes.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A Person comparator orders by score, then id as a deterministic tie-breaker, and returns false when comparing an object with itself. Your goal is to design and test a comparator for sorting and associative lookup. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Iterators and invalidation; Overloading and default arguments; Lambdas and captures; Operator overloading. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: Ordering algorithms and containers require a comparator that behaves as a strict weak ordering, including irreflexivity and transitive equivalence classes. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Using <=, mutable external state, NaN-sensitive relations, or inconsistent tie handling can violate requirements and make behavior undefined or incorrect. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: A sorting comparator should return true for equal elements so either order is accepted.

includes: Supply strict weak orderings and avoid inconsistent comparison behavior.
Design and test a comparator for sorting and associative lookup.
excludes: Using <=, mutable external state, NaN-sensitive relations, or inconsistent tie handling can violate requirements and make behavior undefined or incorrect.

outcomes: Design and test a comparator for sorting and associative lookup.

examples.title: Comparators and ordering: defined case
examples.situation: A Person comparator orders by score, then id as a deterministic tie-breaker, and returns false when comparing an object with itself.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A Person comparator orders by score, then id as a deterministic tie-breaker, and returns false when comparing an object with itself.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: A strict comparator must return false for equivalent values in both directions; use < semantics and consistent tie-breakers.
examples.result: The defined result follows because A strict comparator must return false for equivalent values in both directions; use < semantics and consistent tie-breakers.

examples.title: Comparators and ordering: boundary case
examples.situation: A sort comparator using a <= b is rejected because equal values make comp(a,a) true.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A sort comparator using a <= b is rejected because equal values make comp(a,a) true.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Using <=, mutable external state, NaN-sensitive relations, or inconsistent tie handling can violate requirements and make behavior undefined or incorrect.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: A sorting comparator should return true for equal elements so either order is accepted.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Design and test a comparator for sorting and associative lookup.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A sorting comparator should return true for equal elements so either order is accepted.
misconceptions.correction: A strict comparator must return false for equivalent values in both directions; use < semantics and consistent tie-breakers.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: A sorting comparator should return true for equal elements so either order is accepted.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Comparators and ordering: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Using <=, mutable external state, NaN-sensitive relations, or inconsistent tie handling can violate requirements and make behavior undefined or incorrect.

recap.summary: You should now be able to design and test a comparator for sorting and associative lookup. Keep this boundary in view: Using <=, mutable external state, NaN-sensitive relations, or inconsistent tie handling can violate requirements and make behavior undefined or incorrect.
